sentences.text: Yeah.
In this time, the media was not so much involved in-- invented, I should say, like now.
So what happened, we had some newspapers.
So some newspaper went once a day, some newspaper, once a week.
It was not so disinvolved, the communication.
But we had televisions also, which we could have something before the newspaper brings out.
But in our home, well, very seldom people had it.
Especially the religious people.
Nobody had television.
We didn't believe in it.
Not we.
They, I repeat, have-- they didn't believe it is necessary, or even radio.
They-- it was a-- the religious people was strictly against this.
We know it later, but we know it is coming.
Like I mentioned before, we knew it.
And also, we didn't thought this will be so terrible like- really it happened.
We didn't believe human being could kill-- I should say, in a word, eat human beings.
Like after, you find out exactly on our own skin.
People was driven into ghettos when the war start.
The war took only a few days before-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] when it started, it took a few days there, two days there.
And we're in Poland.
And when the German were in Poland, it was already too late.
You should be-- you should be able-- some people escaped.
Some people.
But once in billions-- I wouldn't say millions-- would escape.
They run away from home, and it-- it happened.
But very seldom.
When the war started, the Germans-- the Germans came in from all the sides.
But very first, as probably you know, we-- Poland has their own army, very well equipped.
In one day, they break-- they-- the Germans was all over Poland.
It took only one day, or two days maximum.
They were-- they come from all the sides.
And they have-- there was equipped, the-- is the-- Polish army was equipped with helicopters.
We didn't have those planes.
But no plane picked up from there and took on fight.
Because in the army, we have a lot of-- in Poland, as I said, you know, at [INAUDIBLE], we have a lot the Germans.
And the Germans, probably they were spies.
And we have big generals which they were-- were descended from German.
And they didn't let the-- their-- their soldiers should go and fight.
Some fought, but it was not equipped.
Poland was not equipped like the Germans.
So Poland was too weak to German.
And with Poland, they got some richness and whatever it is, the Germans.
And they could fight all around the world.
In-- when they came in our town, they start immediately this Jews world wear a Jewish star.
Later, we had to wear a band-- a yellow band.
Later, they make it.
It was very fast.
They leaders of the Jews, they find out who was a leader of the Jews.
Simple to put them in-- in the-- kill them.
Every day, you had another hanging.
They didn't even want to spend the bullet.
They hanged in the middle of the city.
This all the people, Jews especially.
And this time was not yet the ghetto.
They were leaders from the community.
Leader from community, rich people.
The most of them leaders of the community.
They-- they knew everything, because we had a lot of Germans, which, later on, it was-- Poland didn't know this, we have so much-- they spied.
It was a book out of this.
They spied for years to have everything to go in easy to Poland.
Immediately they came.
They came, took away the business, and took-- a few days later, they-- my brothers, all of them.
Now, [INAUDIBLE] was the house.
And they took my mother and father to the Kripo-- this means the criminal police-- and me.
And my father, they cut off the beard this way, which, a religious man, the beard is very important.
So they cut off.
And they left a-- a piece like this hanging.
And they-- they came.
They took away from the house everything what they wanted.
And I was also-- they picked me.
I was the only one at home in this time.
The rest of the brothers run away one-- was not in the house.
And they ask me where my parents has hidden the gold.
I said, I was in school.
I don't know nothing.
What's mean gold?
That's where-- and start to cry.
Put a revolver-- this was in the criminal-- on the desk and said, if you wouldn't say, I'll kill you.
I was crying.
So they call up somebody.
He was a worker in the-- Kriminalpolizei.
He was Jewish.
Later on, I get to know him.
So he said, don't cry.
You will go home.
Don't worry.
And I ask about my mother.
My mother went [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] to a hospital.
They didn't beat her, not my father.
Only they cut off the beard.
And he told me, your mother's in the hospital.
And she got [INAUDIBLE] I don't know.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And the same day-- I was there two nights, I think.
The next day, I went home.
I was alone at home.
The rest of the brothers run away.
They went to Warsaw.
They run away.
And I was the youngest.
And I was at home.
And this was-- it start the-- the ghetto.
So in this way, it start the ghetto.
My mother came home.
Yes, by us.
I think-- I'm sure that you have to write the religion.
So they-- they had all the papers.
They knew it.
And also, there was a lot of the Germans in our town which did cooperate with the Germans.
They forgot their Jewish friends and things like this.
It happened once in-- that some friends, which they were German, was trying to help.
But they was afraid for themself.
They burnt.
Simple.
And one day, when they-- immediately, when they came in-- we had a beautiful synagogue.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
So they-- they burn it.
They put in dynamite and burn.
All the synagogue was probably in their first week.
All of them.
We had beautiful synagogues.
Something like this in [INAUDIBLE].
Was there a curfew established?
Of course.
It was a curfew established immediately.
First of all, was a curfew for all the people, Polish, not Polish.
You couldn't go in the night in the street.
Later, when they put us in the ghetto-- so we was around with the-- with wire, and Germans was watching the ghetto.
And one side was the police-- the Jewish police.
They only as a symbol get the police.
In the other side, the Germans.
But who-- some people escape, but some people got killed.
They ran out of the ghetto.
Because they get out of the ghetto.
They know it.
They didn't need to ask, because around the wire was Germans, soldiers with the-- they were still watching as we should not leave the ghetto.
It was not allowed.
We was living in the-- where we was living, this was path for the ghetto.
Yes, only we had to give away.
We stay only with one room from five.
The rest we have to give away.
And it was uncomfortable.
But this is-- we have a-- a friend here.
They used to come in ghetto.
Schneiderman.
You know him.
His wife.
She used to come in the ghetto to our house.
From five rooms, he was living in one room.
You ask me how do I feel.
I would say we was already dead people.
And we didn't have any feeling.
We couldn't get up and do different.
What you got, though, the-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we was lucky that I still had my father and my mother.
And later, the brothers came back-- two brothers was married-- and sister.
We was close together till '44, when it-- Auschwitz. '43, my father died in--
A lack of, I should say, vitamins.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Still, I had more than somebody else in ghetto.
I was privileged to work in-- in distribution of food.
So I got extra food.
But was not enough.
Was not fed.
And he died from a lack of vitamins.
My mother came to Auschwitz.
Yes.
But we sold.
We tried.
We had some.
I remember we sold.
And we bought-- you could buy, I would say, on the black market, bread, or margarine, whatever it is.
And especially-- our house was a kasher house-- kosher house.
We didn't eat treyfe till-- my father in his life never ate treyfe.
And we got-- how do I say?
Once in a while, a month or what, we got a ration.
This mean on the cards-- I don't know.
Either once a month or once in-- when the Germans put in horse meat.
So you got, let me see, 50 gram per person horse meat.
My father wouldn't eat this.
We had-- I want to [INAUDIBLE] out-- at the lack of vitamins, which you need.
He was an elderly person.
He died.
My mother came to Auschwitz.
Was your family able to keep up with the Jewish calendar?
The holidays?
Yes, as-- the holidays?
Yes, as much-- the holiday was not I should say [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] living-- food.
I should say we didn't have this-- what we had before, let me see, Rosh Hashanah, or Sukkot, or whatever it is.
But the tradition we continue.
No, it was not allowed.
But we people find a way to get together to pray.
It was-- allowed it wasn't.
But we had some stiebels there where we went, next house from my way.
We didn't have shuls anymore.
But we had stiebel, if you know what mean a stiebel.
A small-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] like a synagogue, but very small, like a private room.
Bathroom, no.
We didn't have the bathroom.
It was not like this time in life.
Was bathroom on the yard.
How we say, the--
Outside.
The yard.
We was on the second floor.
You have to go down From the house.
And there was bathrooms.
Yeah, we had-- we had a stove.
In this room, we got in a small stove on the [INAUDIBLE], because all the room-- the kitchen we gave away.
We got the one little-- the front room, we had, with a balcony.
And there we had a little kitchen.
No.
In the ghetto, you have hours of going-- let me see.
Till 5 o'clock.
After 5:00, you couldn't go out.
There was a wire.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
Germans.
Yeah, we had Jewish police in the ghetto.
And by each exit to the city was Germans and the Jewish police, because some people had permission to go out of the city for working or other things.
And German came into the-- to the ghetto.
They had the-- the Gestapo, [NON-ENGLISH], in the ghetto.
So they had to come in.
Yes.
The maximum was-- they hadn't too much power.
In the little towns, like in our town, was the leader, a man-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] what was his name?
Well, I won't [INAUDIBLE] myself.
Yeah.
Yeah.
He was, before the-- before the war-- how do I say?
Involved with the charity.
He was not so wealthy as he was in-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] how do we say?
Orphan house.
He was the leader of a orphan house.
I knew him.
And so he was a public man.
Rumkowski was his name.
Chaim Rumkowski.
And he was the leader of the ghetto.
But he couldn't do what he want.
They told him what he'd do.
He do the maximum-- they put-- they bring him to-- on the end, the Germans gave him-- they call it [INAUDIBLE] letter, that if he comes to Auschwitz, they should not kill him.
He will be there separate because they want to make the city without Jews.
Should belong to the Reich, to the Germans.
This was an excuse.
So they brought them down to Auschwitz.
And he had from the Gestapo a letter, this-- he came down with the letter.
They put him immediately to crematorium with his wife.
The whole family.
They didn't-- this was-- I was-- I came after him a day later.
So I heard people said the day before come the leader, because I was hidden a few days.
I told this we will-- why we couldn't stay over to be hidden.
So we went out and they put us-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and they send it-- they came Germans.
They put us up on the train, took us-- they took us to the train, and from the train immediately to Auschwitz.
You know, you heard a lot of things.
Somebody, for example-- maybe I was more understanding.
And personally, I didn't talk to him.
Yeah, I greet him.
He came to see by us for our work.
He had-- was working with me a person, which he owned obligation to him.
And he want to help him.
What is the help?
He couldn't give me my extra bread once in a month, or once a week, or once when I came.
And he says he should come to see him.
And he was this little-- he was from Poznaiski.
A gentile from the-- from Poznaiski.
Poznaiski was, how do I say?
In Europe-- like, here I would say-- how do I say?
The richest people in Europe.
They had factories, textile factories.
And he was a gentile.
He was convert by the German [INAUDIBLE] home in Germany.
His father was already convert.
But since he worked before the war by him, he wants to show his attitude good to him.
But he was an antisemitist, Poznaiski.
He was in ghetto, this friend who knew him.
But with me, we was living very good, because he needs me and I need him.
Should live over-- we thought we're going to live over.
He-- they sent him to Auschwitz the same thing like me.
I was in a distribution of food.
So my department-- everybody got a card.
So with this card, I have to approve to see who could get-- it was, let me see-- every eight day was a bread.
Every 15 day was a ration-- a little sugar, a little oil, a little thing.
Whatever you got was a ration every two weeks.
So we have a card.
The card was of numbers from 1 to 100.
So today, they giving out, let me see, bread at number one.
So they have to stay in the line, because our store had to attend 10,000 people.
And we couldn't tend 10,000 people one day.
So we make a plan.
Should be in two days, three days, or four days, whatever we could.
And the next, the same thing would have before the bread.
But people were starving.
They stay in the line.
They came again [INAUDIBLE] get after tomorrow.
And we have to approve it.
After we finish the day a few hours later, we gave, because we have the leader of the-- of this group who were working was one of the nicest people in Poland from a very rich family, Mazur.
He didn't live over also.
So if you planned, whatever, we got also the bread to give.
We didn't have the bread.
So whatever we could help, we did.
Let them in to get the bread what-- till the last bread.
Tomorrow, they will bring other bread, so they wouldn't take it.
Then we register this for tomorrow, we shouldn't have problems, because they was controlled by the Germans.
You said that everyone in the ghetto got a ration card.
Did that include the children?
Yeah.
When a child was born, was also [INAUDIBLE], he gets a-- a card.
And for somebody die, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it was so bad.
People keep the corpse for weeks they should get a piece of bread.
But when we bury, we have to give back the card.
We get back the card, we receive the report that this person is canceled, is not born alive.
This number of the card.
The name and every name has a number for the card.
It happened.
No, we was in the ghetto.
Very little Germans came in the ghetto-- came in.
But the Jewish police-- we had Jewish police-- was not-- we didn't have dogs to go house by house in this.
But people they do all kind of things to-- it was so bad.
They want to live over.
Yes.
We had a Jewish cemetery, the old Jewish cemetery, which was normal burials.
It was.
It was very strictly-- they controlled it very strictly.
What it was, you know, black market.
Probably, you know?
It was.
Very seldom.
It is-- we had-- we could send out and we could receive.
But the-- from outside, when it came letters-- let me see.
From outside, from America, from where the Germans control it.
It was schools, yeah, but very little.
Very little.
It was very little schools.
And most of the people, schoolchildren, went to work.
They should get a soup.
When you worked, you get by-- in the work hours, let me see, they have a lunchtime, half an hour with this.
So you got a card where you work.
And you could have a soup.
So all the people was anxious to do soup.
So they went to work.
